---
title: "BS: Crossing the Gender Gap"
description: "A field study, conducted from a toilet queue, of who breaks the unwritten rule and crosses to the other side."
author: "Michael Friendly"
date: "2026-06-17"
draft: true
categories: [beach science, social science, humor]
---

<!-- banner-image: images/Bathroom-Lines.webp -->


It is mid-afternoon at the beach, and nature has called. I make my way
to the beach facilities — a modest block of toilets set back from the sand —
and find what I find at every beach, in every country, without exception: a
queue for one side and nothing for the other.

I join the queue. I wait. I watch.

And I notice something interesting: some people, after a moment's hesitation,
simply cross over. They glance left, glance right, perform a small calculation
that is equal parts urgency and social courage, and walk through the other
door. Others — the majority — wait, shifting from foot to foot, unwilling to
breach the convention.

Who crosses? Who waits? And does it depend on who you are?

Isn't this a great moment for Beach Science?

<!-- https://www.michigandaily.com/opinion/bathroom-lines-a-subtle-day-to-day-reminder-of-gender-inequality/ -->

---

## The Asymmetry Problem

First, a structural observation: the queue is almost never symmetric. Women's
facilities consistently have longer queues than men's, at beaches, concerts,
airports, and anywhere else humans congregate in numbers. This is not
accidental — it is designed in, and has been for decades.

The reasons are partly biological (using a toilet takes women longer on
average), partly architectural (facilities are typically allocated equal floor
area rather than equal throughput capacity), and partly social (women tend to
use facilities in groups, which adds time). The result is a systematic
inequity baked into the infrastructure, which means the crossing temptation
is also asymmetric: women face longer queues and therefore more incentive to cross.

Whether they actually do is a different question.

---

## Who Crosses?

**Urgency** is the obvious one. There is presumably a threshold of discomfort
beyond which social norms become negotiable. The interesting scientific
question is not whether urgency predicts crossing — it obviously does — but
where that threshold sits, and whether it differs by person.

**Age** may be the most interesting variable. Younger people might cross more
readily, having grown up in a culture with more fluid attitudes toward gender
spaces. Older people might be more norm-bound — or, alternatively, more
pragmatic. A 70-year-old who has been waiting for four minutes may have
precisely zero patience left for social convention. The relationship with age
is probably not linear.

**Gender** shapes the social calculus differently for each side. A woman
entering the men's side is doing something that most observers would consider
reasonable — perhaps even sensible — given the queue differential. A man
entering the women's side carries a heavier social cost, regardless of his
intentions. The crossing is the same physical act with very different
social meanings.

**Social context** matters enormously. Crossing alone requires personal
courage; crossing with a friend who says "come on, it's fine" requires
almost none. Friends provide social permission, distributed responsibility,
and someone to laugh with if it goes wrong. Solo travellers cross less.

**Cultural background** is a significant covariate. Attitudes toward gender
segregation in public facilities vary widely by culture, religion, and
national context. A study at a French Mediterranean beach — where attitudes
are generally relaxed — might look very different from the same study at a
different kind of beach in a different kind of country.

---

## The Game Theory of the First Crosser

Here is the dynamic that makes this genuinely interesting as a social
phenomenon: the first person to cross bears all the social cost. Every
subsequent crosser free-rides on the norm violation the first person already
committed.

Once one person crosses, the door is open in more than the literal sense.
Others see that nothing terrible happened, that no one was ejected or
publicly shamed, and their own threshold drops. You would expect cascades —
a burst of crossings following a single pioneer — especially in queues of
strangers who can observe each other.

This is, in miniature, how social norms shift. Someone decides the convention
is less important than the immediate reality, absorbs the initial cost, and
in doing so changes the calculus for everyone behind them. Beach toilets as
a model of social change. BS at its finest.

---

## How Would You Study This?

Naturalistic observation is the method — no intervention, no survey, just
a researcher with a notebook and a clear sightline. Record for each person:

- **Perceived gender** (as presented — the observable variable)
- **Estimated age group** (under 30 / 30–50 / 50+)
- **Queue length on their side** at moment of arrival
- **Queue length on opposite side**
- **Whether they crossed** (binary outcome)
- **Whether they were alone or in a group**
- **Whether a crossing had occurred in the previous two minutes** (the cascade predictor)

Logistic regression with crossing as the binary outcome; queue length
differential, age group, gender, and recent cascade as predictors. An
interaction between gender and queue length would test whether women and men
have different crossing thresholds.

The main methodological challenge is keeping a straight face while explaining
to your companion what you are doing and why.

---

## A Prediction

My beach observation, entirely unscientific, is that women cross more than
men, that the over-60s cross more than anyone thinks, and that the cascade
effect is real and fast — one crossing can empty a queue in under two minutes
once the permission has been granted.

I was not in a position to test this rigorously. I had a queue to join.

---

*A full study of this phenomenon would require ethics approval, a research
assistant, and considerable patience. Or just a hot afternoon and a long
queue, which Leucate Plage reliably provides.*
